lessons.title: Infrastructure as Code
objectives: Use Terraform to provision GPU cloud infrastructure
Create reusable Terraform modules for HPC clusters
Manage Terraform state safely with remote backends
Provision GPU instances on AWS, GCP, and Azure
lessons.content_md:
# Infrastructure as Code for HPC and AI

Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is the practice of managing and provisioning computing
infrastructure through machine-readable configuration files rather than manual processes
or interactive tools. For HPC and AI workloads, IaC is essential because GPU clusters
are expensive, complex, and must be reproducible across development, staging, and
production environments.

## Why IaC Matters for AI Infrastructure

Manual provisioning of GPU clusters is error-prone and slow. A single training cluster
might involve dozens of GPU instances, high-speed networking (EFA on AWS, GPUDirect on
GCP), shared filesystems, security groups, IAM roles, and monitoring agents. IaC captures
all of this in version-controlled files, enabling teams to spin up identical environments
in minutes rather than days.

## Terraform Fundamentals

Terraform by HashiCorp is the most widely adopted IaC tool. It uses a declarative
language called HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) and supports every major cloud
provider.

```hcl
# main.tf вҖ” Provision a GPU training cluster on AWS
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.6.0"
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
    key            = "gpu-cluster/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
    encrypt        = true
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region
}

resource "aws_instance" "gpu_worker" {
  count         = var.worker_count
  ami           = data.aws_ami.deep_learning.id
  instance_type = "p4d.24xlarge"  # 8x A100 80GB GPUs

  subnet_id              = aws_subnet.training.id
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.cluster.id]

  root_block_device {
    volume_size = 500
    volume_type = "gp3"
    throughput  = 500
    iops        = 6000
  }

  # Enable Elastic Fabric Adapter for NCCL
  network_interface {
    device_index          = 1
    network_interface_id  = aws_network_interface.efa[count.index].id
  }

  user_data = templatefile("${path.module}/scripts/setup.sh", {
    efs_dns = aws_efs_file_system.shared_data.dns_name
    role    = "worker"
    index   = count.index
  })

  tags = {
    Name        = "gpu-worker-${count.index}"
    Environment = var.environment
    Team        = "ml-platform"
    CostCenter  = "ai-training"
  }
}

data "aws_ami" "deep_learning" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["amazon"]
  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["Deep Learning AMI GPU PyTorch *"]
  }
}

resource "aws_efs_file_system" "shared_data" {
  creation_token   = "training-data-${var.environment}"
  performance_mode = "maxIO"
  throughput_mode  = "elastic"
  encrypted        = true
  tags = { Name = "training-data-${var.environment}" }
}

resource "aws_efs_mount_target" "shared_data" {
  file_system_id  = aws_efs_file_system.shared_data.id
  subnet_id       = aws_subnet.training.id
  security_groups = [aws_security_group.efs.id]
}
```

## Essential Terraform Commands

```bash
terraform init          # Download providers and initialize backend
terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan  # Preview changes, save plan
terraform apply plan.tfplan      # Apply saved plan exactly
terraform destroy       # Tear down all managed resources
terraform state list    # List resources tracked in state
terraform import aws_instance.gpu_worker[0] i-1234567890abcdef0
```

:::interactive{component="TerminalSimulator" config='{"mode":"linux"}'}

## Modules for Reusability

Terraform modules let you package infrastructure patterns for reuse across teams
and environments. A well-designed module for GPU clusters abstracts away cloud-specific
details while exposing the knobs that matter.

```hcl
# modules/gpu-cluster/variables.tf
variable "cluster_name"  { type = string }
variable "worker_count"  { type = number, default = 4 }
variable "gpu_type"      { type = string, default = "p4d.24xlarge" }
variable "region"        { type = string }
variable "spot_enabled"  { type = bool, default = false }
variable "max_spot_price" { type = string, default = "15.00" }

# Root module usage
module "training_cluster" {
  source       = "./modules/gpu-cluster"
  cluster_name = "llm-training-prod"
  worker_count = 8
  gpu_type     = "p4d.24xlarge"
  region       = "us-east-1"
  spot_enabled = true
}

module "inference_cluster" {
  source       = "./modules/gpu-cluster"
  cluster_name = "inference-prod"
  worker_count = 4
  gpu_type     = "g5.2xlarge"
  region       = "us-west-2"
  spot_enabled = false
}
```

:::interactive{component="NetworkTopologyViewer"}

## State Management Best Practices

Terraform state is the single source of truth mapping your configuration to real
infrastructure. Never store state locally in production. Use a remote backend with
locking (S3 + DynamoDB, or GCS + Cloud Storage) to prevent concurrent modifications.
State contains sensitive data, so always enable encryption.

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  Developer A  в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ   S3 Backend   в”Ӯв—Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ӯ  Developer B в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  terraform    в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  + DynamoDB    в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  terraform   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  apply        в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  (state lock)  в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  apply       в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                             в”Ӯ
                             в–ј (blocks concurrent writes)
```

:::interactive{component="TerminalSimulator" config='{"mode":"linux"}'}

## Multi-Cloud Considerations

For GCP, replace `aws_instance` with `google_compute_instance` and use `a2-highgpu-8g`
(8x A100) or `a3-highgpu-8g` (8x H100). For Azure, use `azurerm_linux_virtual_machine`
with `Standard_ND96amsr_A100_v4`. Each cloud has different GPU availability zones,
networking options (VPC peering, PrivateLink), and pricing models. Terraform's
multi-provider support lets you manage heterogeneous infrastructure from a single
configuration.

exercises.title: Terraform State Management
exercises.description: What is the primary purpose of Terraform state?
options: It speeds up Terraform plan execution by caching provider APIs
It tracks the mapping between configuration resources and real-world infrastructure objects
It stores application logs and deployment history for audit purposes
It compiles HCL code into cloud-specific API calls for faster execution
exercises.correct_answer: It tracks the mapping between configuration resources and real-world infrastructure objects
hints: Without state, Terraform would have no way to know what resources it has already created
Consider what happens when you run terraform plan вҖ” it needs to compare desired vs actual
exercises.solution: Terraform state records the mapping between each resource block in your configuration and the actual infrastructure object it manages (e.g., an AWS instance ID). This allows Terraform to determine what changes are needed during plan, detect drift, and manage the full lifecycle of resources. State should be stored remotely with locking to enable team collaboration.

exercises.title: Write a GPU Cluster Module
exercises.description: Write a Terraform configuration that provisions a variable number of GPU instances with a shared EFS filesystem and placement group for optimal NCCL performance. Include variables for worker_count, instance_type, and environment.
hints: Use aws_instance with count = var.worker_count
Use aws_placement_group with strategy 'cluster' for GPU-to-GPU proximity
Use aws_efs_file_system with performance_mode 'maxIO' for parallel training I/O

exercises.title: Multi-Environment GPU Infrastructure
exercises.description: Design a complete Terraform project structure with separate workspaces for dev, staging, and prod GPU clusters. Create a reusable module that provisions GPU instances, networking, and shared storage. The dev environment should use spot instances with 2 workers, staging should use 4 on-demand instances, and prod should use 8 on-demand instances with placement groups.
hints: Use Terraform workspaces or separate .tfvars files for each environment
Create a modules/gpu-cluster directory with main.tf, variables.tf, and outputs.tf
Use conditional expressions: spot_price = var.spot_enabled ? var.max_spot_price : null
exercises.solution:
# Directory structure:
# в”ңв”Җв”Җ environments/
# в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ dev.tfvars      (worker_count=2, spot_enabled=true)
# в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ staging.tfvars  (worker_count=4, spot_enabled=false)
# в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ prod.tfvars    (worker_count=8, spot_enabled=false, placement_group=true)
# в”ңв”Җв”Җ modules/
# в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ gpu-cluster/
# в”Ӯ       в”ңв”Җв”Җ main.tf
# в”Ӯ       в”ңв”Җв”Җ variables.tf
# в”Ӯ       в””в”Җв”Җ outputs.tf
# в””в”Җв”Җ main.tf

# modules/gpu-cluster/main.tf
resource "aws_placement_group" "cluster" {
  count    = var.use_placement_group ? 1 : 0
  name     = "${var.cluster_name}-pg"
  strategy = "cluster"
}

resource "aws_spot_instance_request" "gpu_spot" {
  count                = var.spot_enabled ? var.worker_count : 0
  ami                  = var.ami_id
  instance_type        = var.instance_type
  spot_price           = var.max_spot_price
  wait_for_fulfillment = true
  tags = { Name = "${var.cluster_name}-spot-${count.index}" }
}

resource "aws_instance" "gpu_ondemand" {
  count           = var.spot_enabled ? 0 : var.worker_count
  ami             = var.ami_id
  instance_type   = var.instance_type
  placement_group = var.use_placement_group ? aws_placement_group.cluster[0].id : null
  tags = { Name = "${var.cluster_name}-${count.index}" }
}

# Usage: terraform apply -var-file=environments/prod.tfvars

lessons.title: MLOps Pipeline Design
objectives: Design end-to-end CI/CD pipelines for ML model lifecycle
Build training workflows with Kubeflow Pipelines
Implement automated training, evaluation, and model promotion
Use Apache Airflow to orchestrate complex ML workflows
lessons.content_md:
# MLOps Pipeline Design

MLOps extends DevOps principles to machine learning, addressing the unique challenges
of ML systems: data dependencies, experimental tracking, model versioning, and the
need to continuously retrain as data distributions shift. A robust MLOps pipeline
automates the entire lifecycle from raw data ingestion to production model deployment.

## The ML Pipeline Lifecycle

Unlike traditional software where code changes drive deployments, ML systems have
three axes of change: code, data, and model configuration. A well-designed MLOps
pipeline tracks all three and automates the transitions between stages.

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   Data       в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ  Feature    в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ   Model      в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ  Model   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Ingestion   в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ Engineering в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  Training    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  Eval    в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                                                               в”Ӯ
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Production  в”Ӯв—Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ӯ   Canary    в”Ӯв—Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ӯ   Model      в”Ӯв—Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
в”Ӯ  Traffic     в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  Rollout    в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  Registry    в”Ӯ   (if metrics pass)
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

:::interactive{component="PipelineBuilder" config='{"preset":"mlops-full"}'}

## Kubeflow Pipelines

Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP) is the de facto standard for Kubernetes-native ML workflows.
Each step runs in its own container, enabling dependency isolation and GPU scheduling.

```python
from kfp import dsl
from kfp.dsl import Input, Output, Dataset, Model, Metrics

@dsl.component(
    base_image="python:3.11-slim",
    packages_to_install=["pandas", "pyarrow", "great-expectations"]
)
def validate_data(
    data_path: str,
    validated_data: Output[Dataset],
    validation_report: Output[Metrics]
):
    """Validate incoming data against quality expectations."""
    import pandas as pd
    import great_expectations as gx

    df = pd.read_parquet(data_path)
    context = gx.get_context()
    results = context.run_checkpoint(checkpoint_name="data_quality")

    if not results.success:
        raise ValueError(f"Data validation failed: {results}")

    df.to_parquet(validated_data.path)
    validation_report.log_metric("row_count", len(df))
    validation_report.log_metric("validation_passed", 1.0)

@dsl.component(
    base_image="nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:24.01-py3",
    packages_to_install=["mlflow", "wandb"]
)
def train_model(
    data: Input[Dataset],
    epochs: int,
    learning_rate: float,
    trained_model: Output[Model],
    metrics: Output[Metrics]
):
    """Train a model on validated data with experiment tracking."""
    import torch
    import mlflow

    mlflow.set_tracking_uri("http://mlflow-server:5000")
    with mlflow.start_run():
        model = build_model()
        optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=learning_rate)

        for epoch in range(epochs):
            train_loss = train_one_epoch(model, data.path, optimizer)
            val_loss, val_acc = evaluate(model, data.path)
            mlflow.log_metrics({
                "train_loss": train_loss,
                "val_loss": val_loss,
                "val_accuracy": val_acc
            }, step=epoch)

        torch.save(model.state_dict(), trained_model.path)
        metrics.log_metric("final_accuracy", val_acc)

@dsl.component(base_image="python:3.11-slim")
def promote_model(
    model: Input[Model],
    accuracy: float,
    threshold: float = 0.95
) -> bool:
    """Promote model to production if it meets quality threshold."""
    if accuracy >= threshold:
        # Register in model registry and trigger canary deployment
        register_model(model.path, stage="production")
        return True
    return False

@dsl.pipeline(
    name="training-pipeline",
    description="End-to-end model training with validation and promotion"
)
def ml_pipeline(
    data_path: str,
    epochs: int = 10,
    learning_rate: float = 1e-4,
    promotion_threshold: float = 0.95
):
    validate_task = validate_data(data_path=data_path)
    train_task = train_model(
        data=validate_task.outputs["validated_data"],
        epochs=epochs,
        learning_rate=learning_rate
    )
    train_task.set_gpu_limit(8)
    train_task.set_memory_limit("128Gi")

    promote_model(
        model=train_task.outputs["trained_model"],
        accuracy=train_task.outputs["metrics"],
        threshold=promotion_threshold
    )
```

## Apache Airflow for Orchestration

For organizations that need more flexibility or already use Airflow, you can
orchestrate ML pipelines with custom operators.

```python
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.providers.cncf.kubernetes.operators.pod import KubernetesPodOperator
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

default_args = {
    "owner": "ml-platform",
    "retries": 2,
    "retry_delay": timedelta(minutes=10),
}

with DAG(
    "daily_retraining",
    default_args=default_args,
    schedule_interval="0 2 * * *",
    start_date=datetime(2024, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
) as dag:
    validate = KubernetesPodOperator(
        task_id="validate_data",
        image="ml-pipeline/validator:v2",
        arguments=["--date", "{{ ds }}"],
        namespace="ml-pipelines",
    )
    train = KubernetesPodOperator(
        task_id="train_model",
        image="ml-pipeline/trainer:v2",
        arguments=["--date", "{{ ds }}", "--epochs", "10"],
        namespace="ml-pipelines",
        container_resources={
            "limits": {"nvidia.com/gpu": "8", "memory": "128Gi"}
        },
    )
    deploy = KubernetesPodOperator(
        task_id="deploy_canary",
        image="ml-pipeline/deployer:v2",
        arguments=["--canary-pct", "10"],
        namespace="ml-pipelines",
    )
    validate >> train >> deploy
```

:::interactive{component="PipelineBuilder" config='{"preset":"ci-cd"}'}

## GitHub Actions CI/CD for ML

CI/CD pipelines validate code changes and trigger retraining automatically.

```yaml
name: ML Pipeline CI/CD
on:
  push:
    paths: ['models/**', 'configs/**', 'data/schemas/**']
jobs:
  test-and-train:
    runs-on: [self-hosted, gpu]
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - name: Run unit tests
      run: pytest tests/ -v --tb=short
    - name: Train model
      run: python train.py --config configs/prod.yaml
    - name: Evaluate against baseline
      run: python evaluate.py --threshold 0.95 --compare-baseline
    - name: Deploy canary if improved
      if: success()
      run: python deploy.py --model latest --canary-pct 10
```

:::interactive{component="GitVisualizer" config='{"preset":"gitflow"}'}

## Model Versioning and Promotion

A model registry acts as the single source of truth for model artifacts. Models
progress through stages: *Experimental вҶ’ Staging вҶ’ Production вҶ’ Archived*. Only
models that pass automated evaluation gates and manual review are promoted.

exercises.title: MLOps vs Traditional DevOps
exercises.description: What fundamentally distinguishes MLOps from traditional DevOps?
options: MLOps does not use version control or CI/CD practices
MLOps must also version data and models alongside code, and handle data-dependent behavior changes
MLOps only works in cloud environments, not on-premises
MLOps replaces all DevOps tools with ML-specific alternatives
exercises.correct_answer: MLOps must also version data and models alongside code, and handle data-dependent behavior changes
hints: Traditional software behavior changes only with code changes, but ML systems change with data too
Consider the three axes of change in ML systems: code, data, and model configuration
exercises.solution: Unlike traditional software where behavior changes only with code, ML systems have three axes of change: code, data, and model configuration. MLOps extends DevOps to track and automate all three. A data distribution shift can degrade model performance without any code change, requiring continuous monitoring, retraining triggers, and automated evaluation gates that don't exist in traditional DevOps.

exercises.title: Kubeflow Training Pipeline
exercises.description: Write a Kubeflow Pipelines pipeline with three components: data_preprocess (CPU), train_model (8 GPUs, 128Gi memory), and evaluate_model (1 GPU). The pipeline should pass data between stages using KFP artifacts and only proceed to evaluation if training completes successfully.
hints: Use @dsl.component decorator with base_image parameter for each step
Use Input/Output type annotations with Dataset and Model artifact types
Set GPU limits with task.set_gpu_limit() after component instantiation

exercises.title: End-to-End MLOps Pipeline with Automated Promotion
exercises.description: Build a complete MLOps pipeline that: (1) validates incoming data with schema checks, (2) trains a model with experiment tracking via MLflow, (3) evaluates the model against the current production baseline, (4) automatically promotes the model if it beats the baseline by at least 2%, and (5) triggers a canary deployment at 10% traffic. Write the Kubeflow pipeline definition and an Airflow DAG that schedules daily retraining.
hints: Use MLflow's model registry API to compare against the current production model
Use Airflow's KubernetesPodOperator to trigger each pipeline stage
Implement a gate function that compares new model accuracy vs production model accuracy
exercises.solution:
# Kubeflow pipeline with automated promotion
from kfp import dsl
from kfp.dsl import Input, Output, Dataset, Model, Metrics

@dsl.component(base_image="python:3.11-slim")
def validate_and_prepare(data_path: str, clean_data: Output[Dataset]):
    import pandas as pd
    df = pd.read_parquet(data_path)
    assert len(df) > 1000, "Insufficient data"
    assert df.isnull().sum().sum() / df.size < 0.05, "Too many nulls"
    df.dropna().to_parquet(clean_data.path)

@dsl.component(base_image="nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:24.01-py3")
def train_with_tracking(data: Input[Dataset], model: Output[Model], metrics: Output[Metrics]):
    import mlflow
    mlflow.set_tracking_uri("http://mlflow:5000")
    with mlflow.start_run():
        acc = train_and_evaluate(data.path, model.path)
        mlflow.log_metric("accuracy", acc)
        metrics.log_metric("accuracy", acc)

@dsl.component(base_image="python:3.11-slim")
def promote_if_better(model: Input[Model], new_accuracy: float, min_improvement: float = 0.02):
    import mlflow
    client = mlflow.tracking.MlflowClient()
    prod = client.get_latest_versions("my-model", stages=["Production"])
    prod_acc = float(client.get_run(prod[0].run_id).data.metrics["accuracy"])
    if new_accuracy >= prod_acc + min_improvement:
        client.transition_model_version_stage("my-model", prod[0].version, "Production")
        trigger_canary_deploy(canary_pct=10)

@dsl.pipeline(name="daily-retraining")
def pipeline(data_path: str):
    val = validate_and_prepare(data_path=data_path)
    train = train_with_tracking(data=val.outputs["clean_data"])
    train.set_gpu_limit(8)
    promote_if_better(model=train.outputs["model"], new_accuracy=train.outputs["metrics"])

Configure dynamic batching and model ensembles for high throughput
Implement autoscaling inference services on Kubernetes with KServe
Design A/B testing and canary deployment strategies for model rollouts
lessons.content_md:
# Model Serving & Inference

Serving ML models in production demands low latency, high throughput, reliability,
and the ability to update models without downtime. The inference stack must handle
bursty traffic, batch requests efficiently on GPUs, and support multiple model
versions simultaneously for safe rollouts.

## NVIDIA Triton Inference Server

Triton is the industry-standard inference server for production ML. It supports
multiple frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow, ONNX, TensorRT, Python), provides
dynamic batching, model ensembles, and fine-grained GPU scheduling.

### Model Repository Structure

```
model_repository/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ text_encoder/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ config.pbtxt
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ 1/
в”Ӯ       в””в”Җв”Җ model.onnx
в”ңв”Җв”Җ reranker/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ config.pbtxt
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ 1/
в”Ӯ       в””в”Җв”Җ model.plan          # TensorRT optimized
в””в”Җв”Җ ensemble_pipeline/
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ config.pbtxt            # Chains text_encoder вҶ’ reranker
    в””в”Җв”Җ 1/
        в””в”Җв”Җ (empty for ensemble)
```

### Triton Model Configuration

```protobuf
# config.pbtxt for an LLM inference model
name: "llm_model"
platform: "onnxruntime_onnx"
max_batch_size: 64

input [{
  name: "input_ids"
  data_type: TYPE_INT64
  dims: [512]
}, {
  name: "attention_mask"
  data_type: TYPE_INT64
  dims: [512]
}]

output [{
  name: "logits"
  data_type: TYPE_FP32
  dims: [512, 32000]
}]

# Dynamic batching groups individual requests into batches
dynamic_batching {
  preferred_batch_size: [8, 16, 32, 64]
  max_queue_delay_microseconds: 200
  preserve_ordering: true
}

# Run 2 model instances per GPU for pipeline parallelism
instance_group [{
  count: 2
  kind: KIND_GPU
  gpus: [0, 1]
}]

# Model warmup to avoid cold-start latency
model_warmup [{
  name: "warmup_requests"
  batch_size: 8
  inputs {
    key: "input_ids"
    value: { data_type: TYPE_INT64, dims: [512], zero_data: true }
  }
}]
```

### Launching Triton

```bash
# Launch Triton with GPU support and all three protocols
docker run --gpus all --rm \
    -p 8000:8000 \  # HTTP
    -p 8001:8001 \  # gRPC
    -p 8002:8002 \  # Metrics (Prometheus)
    -v $(pwd)/model_repository:/models \
    nvcr.io/nvidia/tritonserver:24.05-py3 \
    tritonserver \
        --model-repository=/models \
        --log-verbose=1 \
        --strict-model-config=false
```

:::interactive{component="TerminalSimulator" config='{"mode":"linux"}'}

### Python Client for Triton

```python
import tritonclient.http as httpclient
import numpy as np

client = httpclient.InferenceServerClient(url="localhost:8000")

# Check model readiness
assert client.is_model_ready("llm_model")

# Prepare inputs
input_ids = httpclient.InferInput("input_ids", [1, 512], "INT64")
input_ids.set_data_from_numpy(np.zeros((1, 512), dtype=np.int64))

attention_mask = httpclient.InferInput("attention_mask", [1, 512], "INT64")
attention_mask.set_data_from_numpy(np.ones((1, 512), dtype=np.int64))

# Run inference
result = client.infer(
    model_name="llm_model",
    inputs=[input_ids, attention_mask],
    outputs=[httpclient.InferRequestedOutput("logits")]
)
logits = result.as_numpy("logits")
print(f"Output shape: {logits.shape}")  # (1, 512, 32000)
```

## TorchServe

TorchServe is PyTorch's native serving solution, ideal for teams deeply invested
in the PyTorch ecosystem.

```python
# handler.py вҖ” Custom TorchServe handler
from ts.torch_handler.base_handler import BaseHandler
import torch

class LLMHandler(BaseHandler):
    def initialize(self, context):
        self.model = torch.load(context.model_dir + "/model.pt")
        self.model.eval().cuda()

    def preprocess(self, requests):
        return [self.tokenizer(r["body"]["text"]) for r in requests]

    def inference(self, inputs):
        with torch.no_grad(), torch.cuda.amp.autocast():
            return self.model(inputs)

    def postprocess(self, outputs):
        return [{"predictions": o.cpu().tolist()} for o in outputs]
```

## KServe on Kubernetes

KServe provides a Kubernetes-native way to deploy inference services with
autoscaling, canary rollouts, and traffic splitting.

```yaml
apiVersion: serving.kserve.io/v1beta1
kind: InferenceService
metadata:
  name: llm-service
  annotations:
    serving.kserve.io/autoscalerClass: hpa
    serving.kserve.io/targetUtilizationPercentage: "70"
spec:
  predictor:
    minReplicas: 2
    maxReplicas: 20
    model:
      modelFormat:
        name: pytorch
      resources:
        limits:
          nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
          memory: "32Gi"
        requests:
          nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
          memory: "16Gi"
      storageUri: "s3://models/llm-v2"
    canaryTrafficPercent: 10
```

:::interactive{component="K8sVisualizer"}

## A/B Testing and Canary Deployments

Safe model rollouts use traffic splitting to gradually shift load from the old
model to the new one while monitoring key metrics.

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ     90% traffic     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  Ingress  в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ  Model v1    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Gateway  в”Ӯ                     в”Ӯ  (baseline)  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ           в”Ӯ     10% traffic     в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ           в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ  Model v2    в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                      в”Ӯ  (canary)    в”Ӯ
                                  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

:::interactive{component="NetworkTopologyViewer"}

exercises.title: Dynamic Batching Benefits
exercises.description: What is the primary benefit of dynamic batching in NVIDIA Triton Inference Server?
options: It reduces the size of model weights to save GPU memory
It groups individual inference requests into batches to maximize GPU utilization and throughput
It distributes requests across multiple physical servers for load balancing
It compresses the input tensors to reduce network transfer time
exercises.correct_answer: It groups individual inference requests into batches to maximize GPU utilization and throughput
hints: GPUs are massively parallel processors вҖ” processing one sample at a time wastes most of the compute capacity
Think about what happens when requests arrive one at a time vs in groups
exercises.solution: Dynamic batching in Triton collects individual inference requests that arrive within a configurable time window (max_queue_delay_microseconds) and combines them into a single batch for GPU execution. Since GPUs are designed for parallel processing, running a batch of 32 requests takes only marginally longer than a single request, effectively multiplying throughput. The preferred_batch_size parameter lets you specify optimal batch sizes for your specific model and GPU.

exercises.title: Triton Model Configuration
exercises.description: Write a complete Triton config.pbtxt for serving a BERT-based text classification model. The model takes input_ids (INT64, max sequence length 256) and attention_mask (INT64, max length 256), outputs class_probabilities (FP32, 5 classes). Configure dynamic batching with preferred batch sizes of 8 and 32, max queue delay of 150 microseconds, and 2 GPU instances.
hints: Use platform: onnxruntime_onnx for ONNX models
Set max_batch_size to define the upper bound on batch dimension
Each instance_group entry specifies count and kind (KIND_GPU)

exercises.title: Production Inference Service with Canary Deployment
exercises.description: Design a complete production inference stack: (1) Write a Triton config.pbtxt with dynamic batching and model warmup, (2) Create a KServe InferenceService YAML with autoscaling (min 2, max 20 replicas, target 70% GPU utilization), (3) Write a Python client that sends batched requests and measures p50/p95/p99 latencies, and (4) Configure a canary deployment that routes 10% of traffic to the new model version.
hints: Use tritonclient.http for the Python client with concurrent requests
KServe supports canaryTrafficPercent in the predictor spec
Measure latency with time.perf_counter() around each inference call and use numpy percentiles
exercises.solution:
# 1. Triton config.pbtxt
# name: "my_model"
# platform: "onnxruntime_onnx"
# max_batch_size: 64
# dynamic_batching { preferred_batch_size: [16, 32] max_queue_delay_microseconds: 100 }
# instance_group [{ count: 2, kind: KIND_GPU }]

# 2. KServe InferenceService
# apiVersion: serving.kserve.io/v1beta1
# kind: InferenceService
# metadata:
#   name: my-service
#   annotations:
#     serving.kserve.io/autoscalerClass: hpa
#     serving.kserve.io/targetUtilizationPercentage: "70"
# spec:
#   predictor:
#     minReplicas: 2
#     maxReplicas: 20
#     canaryTrafficPercent: 10
#     model:
#       modelFormat: { name: triton }
#       storageUri: "s3://models/v2"
#       resources:
#         limits: { nvidia.com/gpu: "1" }

# 3. Python latency benchmark client
import tritonclient.http as httpclient
import numpy as np
import time
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

client = httpclient.InferenceServerClient("localhost:8000")
latencies = []

def send_request():
    inp = httpclient.InferInput("input_ids", [1, 256], "INT64")
    inp.set_data_from_numpy(np.zeros((1, 256), dtype=np.int64))
    start = time.perf_counter()
    client.infer("my_model", [inp])
    latencies.append(time.perf_counter() - start)

with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=16) as pool:
    list(pool.map(lambda _: send_request(), range(1000)))

arr = np.array(latencies) * 1000
print(f"p50={np.percentile(arr,50):.1f}ms p95={np.percentile(arr,95):.1f}ms p99={np.percentile(arr,99):.1f}ms")

lessons.content_md:
# Monitoring & Observability for AI Infrastructure

You cannot optimize what you cannot measure. For GPU-intensive AI workloads,
monitoring is critical for maximizing utilization (GPUs cost $2-30/hour each),
detecting training failures early, ensuring inference SLAs, and controlling costs.
A comprehensive observability stack covers infrastructure metrics, application
metrics, logs, and traces.

## The GPU Monitoring Stack

The standard production stack for GPU monitoring consists of three layers:

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                    Grafana                           в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ            (Dashboards & Visualization)              в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                      в”Ӯ PromQL queries
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                  Prometheus                          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ          (Time-series DB & Alerting)                 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
       в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ               в”Ӯ
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ    DCGM     в”Ӯ в”Ӯ   Node      в”Ӯ в”Ӯ  Application    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   Exporter  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ  Exporter   в”Ӯ в”Ӯ  Metrics        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ (GPU stats) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (CPU/mem)   в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (/metrics)      в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

:::interactive{component="NetworkTopologyViewer"}

## DCGM Exporter Setup

NVIDIA's Data Center GPU Manager (DCGM) Exporter runs as a DaemonSet on every
GPU node, scraping detailed GPU metrics and exposing them in Prometheus format.

```yaml
# dcgm-exporter DaemonSet for Kubernetes
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: dcgm-exporter
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    app: dcgm-exporter
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: dcgm-exporter
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: dcgm-exporter
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
        prometheus.io/port: "9400"
    spec:
      nodeSelector:
        nvidia.com/gpu.present: "true"
      tolerations:
      - key: nvidia.com/gpu
        operator: Exists
        effect: NoSchedule
      containers:
      - name: dcgm-exporter
        image: nvcr.io/nvidia/k8s/dcgm-exporter:3.3.5-3.4.1-ubuntu22.04
        ports:
        - name: metrics
          containerPort: 9400
        env:
        - name: DCGM_EXPORTER_KUBERNETES
          value: "true"
        - name: DCGM_EXPORTER_LISTEN
          value: ":9400"
        securityContext:
          runAsNonRoot: false
          privileged: true
        volumeMounts:
        - name: nvidia-install-dir-host
          mountPath: /usr/local/nvidia
      volumes:
      - name: nvidia-install-dir-host
        hostPath:
          path: /usr/local/nvidia
          type: Directory
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: dcgm-exporter
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    app: dcgm-exporter
spec:
  selector:
    app: dcgm-exporter
  ports:
  - port: 9400
    targetPort: 9400
    name: metrics
```

:::interactive{component="K8sVisualizer"}

## Key GPU Metrics

| Metric Name | Description | Healthy Range |
|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| `DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL` | GPU compute utilization % | > 80% during training |
| `DCGM_FI_DEV_MEM_COPY_UTIL` | Memory bandwidth utilization % | Varies by workload |
| `DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED` | Framebuffer (GPU) memory used (MB) | < 95% of total |
| `DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_FREE` | Framebuffer memory free (MB) | > 5% of total |
| `DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_TEMP` | GPU temperature (В°C) | < 83В°C sustained |
| `DCGM_FI_DEV_POWER_USAGE` | Power consumption (W) | < TDP rating |
| `DCGM_FI_DEV_SM_CLOCK` | Streaming multiprocessor clock (MHz) | Near max boost |
| `DCGM_FI_DEV_XID_ERRORS` | XID error count | Must be 0 |
| `DCGM_FI_DEV_PCIE_TX_THROUGHPUT` | PCIe transmit throughput | Varies |
| `DCGM_FI_DEV_NVLINK_BANDWIDTH_TOTAL` | NVLink total bandwidth | Near max for multi-GPU |

## Prometheus Alerting Rules

```yaml
# prometheus-gpu-alerts.yaml
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: gpu-alerts
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  groups:
  - name: gpu.rules
    rules:
    - alert: GPUUtilizationLow
      expr: avg_over_time(DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL[10m]) < 50
      for: 15m
      labels:
        severity: warning
        team: ml-platform
      annotations:
        summary: "GPU {{ $labels.gpu }} on {{ $labels.instance }} underutilized at {{ $value }}%"
        runbook_url: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/gpu-underutilized"

    - alert: GPUMemoryNearOOM
      expr: (DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED / (DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED + DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_FREE)) * 100 > 95
      for: 5m
      labels:
        severity: critical
        team: ml-platform
      annotations:
        summary: "GPU {{ $labels.gpu }} memory at {{ $value }}% вҖ” OOM risk"

    - alert: GPUTemperatureHigh
      expr: DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_TEMP > 85
      for: 5m
      labels:
        severity: critical
      annotations:
        summary: "GPU {{ $labels.gpu }} temperature {{ $value }}В°C exceeds threshold"

    - alert: GPUXIDErrors
      expr: increase(DCGM_FI_DEV_XID_ERRORS[5m]) > 0
      labels:
        severity: critical
      annotations:
        summary: "GPU {{ $labels.gpu }} XID errors detected вҖ” potential hardware failure"
```

:::interactive{component="TerminalSimulator" config='{"mode":"linux"}'}

## SLOs and SLIs for ML Services

Define Service Level Objectives (SLOs) based on measurable Service Level
Indicators (SLIs):

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ SLI                 в”Ӯ Measurement           в”Ӯ SLO Target     в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Availability        в”Ӯ successful / total    в”Ӯ 99.9%          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Latency (p99)       в”Ӯ response time         в”Ӯ < 200ms        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Throughput          в”Ӯ requests/sec          в”Ӯ > 1000 rps     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Error rate          в”Ӯ 5xx / total           в”Ӯ < 0.1%         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Model freshness     в”Ӯ time since retrain    в”Ӯ < 24 hours     в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

## PromQL Queries for ML Dashboards

```promql
# GPU utilization across all nodes (average)
avg(DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL) by (instance)

# Memory usage percentage per GPU
100 * DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED / (DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED + DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_FREE)

# Inference p99 latency
histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(inference_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))

# Error budget remaining (30-day window, 99.9% SLO)
1 - ((1 - (sum(rate(http_requests_total{code!~"5.."}[30d])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total[30d])))) / 0.001)
```

:::interactive{component="GuidedLab"}

exercises.title: DCGM Exporter Purpose
exercises.description: What is DCGM and what role does the DCGM Exporter play in GPU monitoring?
options: DCGM (Distributed Computing Grid Manager) manages compute grids and the exporter distributes jobs
DCGM (Data Center GPU Manager) is NVIDIA's GPU management tool and the exporter exposes GPU metrics in Prometheus format
DCGM (Docker Container GPU Monitor) monitors GPU usage in containers and exports logs to Elasticsearch
DCGM (Deep Compute General Manager) optimizes deep learning and exports training metrics to TensorBoard
exercises.correct_answer: DCGM (Data Center GPU Manager) is NVIDIA's GPU management tool and the exporter exposes GPU metrics in Prometheus format
hints: DCGM is an official NVIDIA tool designed specifically for data center GPU fleet management
The exporter bridges DCGM metrics into the Prometheus ecosystem
exercises.solution: DCGM (Data Center GPU Manager) is NVIDIA's official suite for managing and monitoring GPUs in data center environments. It provides APIs and tools for health monitoring, diagnostics, power and clock management, and process accounting. The DCGM Exporter is a container that runs as a Kubernetes DaemonSet on GPU nodes, collecting DCGM metrics (GPU utilization, memory usage, temperature, power, errors) and exposing them on port 9400 in Prometheus exposition format, enabling scraping by Prometheus and visualization in Grafana.

exercises.title: Prometheus Alerting Rules for GPU Cluster
exercises.description: Write a complete PrometheusRule resource (Kubernetes CRD) with four alert rules: (1) GPUUtilizationLow вҖ” fires when average GPU utilization drops below 40% for 15 minutes (warning), (2) GPUMemoryExhausted вҖ” fires when GPU memory usage exceeds 95% for 5 minutes (critical), (3) GPUTemperatureCritical вҖ” fires when GPU temperature exceeds 85В°C (critical), (4) InferenceLatencyHigh вҖ” fires when p99 inference latency exceeds 500ms for 10 minutes (warning). Include proper labels and annotations.
hints: Use monitoring.coreos.com/v1 PrometheusRule CRD kind
DCGM metric for utilization is DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL
Use histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(...)) for p99 latency
exercises.solution:
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: gpu-cluster-alerts
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  groups:
  - name: gpu.alerts
    rules:
    - alert: GPUUtilizationLow
      expr: avg_over_time(DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL[15m]) < 40
      for: 15m
      labels:
        severity: warning
      annotations:
        summary: "GPU {{ $labels.gpu }} underutilized at {{ $value }}%"

    - alert: GPUMemoryExhausted
      expr: (DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED / (DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED + DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_FREE)) * 100 > 95
      for: 5m
      labels:
        severity: critical
      annotations:
        summary: "GPU {{ $labels.gpu }} memory at {{ $value }}% вҖ” OOM imminent"

    - alert: GPUTemperatureCritical
      expr: DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_TEMP > 85
      for: 2m
      labels:
        severity: critical
      annotations:
        summary: "GPU {{ $labels.gpu }} temperature {{ $value }}В°C"

    - alert: InferenceLatencyHigh
      expr: histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(inference_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) > 0.5
      for: 10m
      labels:
        severity: warning
      annotations:
        summary: "Inference p99 latency {{ $value }}s exceeds 500ms SLO"

lessons.title: Ray & Dask for Scalable Computing
objectives: Build distributed applications with Ray remote functions and actors
Use Ray Train for distributed model training across GPU clusters
Deploy scalable inference services with Ray Serve
Process large-scale datasets with Dask distributed DataFrames and arrays
Design task graphs and understand scaling patterns for compute-bound workloads
lessons.content_md:
# Ray & Dask for Scalable Computing

While MPI and NCCL provide low-level building blocks for distributed computing,
Ray and Dask offer higher-level abstractions that simplify building scalable AI
applications. Ray excels at heterogeneous distributed computing (training, serving,
data processing, reinforcement learning), while Dask specializes in scaling familiar
Python data structures (DataFrames, arrays) to cluster-scale.

## Ray Architecture

Ray is a general-purpose distributed computing framework built around three
core primitives: **tasks** (stateless functions), **actors** (stateful classes),
and **objects** (immutable values in shared memory).

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

в”Ӯ                                                     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ      в”Ӯ
```

## Ray Remote Functions and Actors

```python
import ray
import torch
import numpy as np

# Initialize Ray cluster
ray.init(address="auto")  # Connect to existing cluster
print(f"Cluster resources: {ray.cluster_resources()}")

# Remote function вҖ” stateless, runs on any worker
@ray.remote(num_gpus=1)
def train_fold(data_ref, fold_idx: int, config: dict):
    """Train a model on one fold of cross-validation."""
    gpu_id = ray.get_gpu_ids()[0]
    device = torch.device(f"cuda:{gpu_id}")

    data = ray.get(data_ref)
    train_data, val_data = split_fold(data, fold_idx, n_folds=5)

    model = build_model(config).to(device)
    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=config["lr"])

    for epoch in range(config["epochs"]):
        train_loss = train_epoch(model, train_data, optimizer, device)
        val_loss, val_acc = evaluate(model, val_data, device)

    return {"fold": fold_idx, "val_acc": val_acc, "val_loss": val_loss}

# Launch 5-fold cross-validation in parallel across 5 GPUs
dataset = load_dataset("training_data.parquet")
data_ref = ray.put(dataset)  # Store once in shared object store

config = {"lr": 1e-4, "epochs": 20, "hidden_dim": 512}
futures = [train_fold.remote(data_ref, i, config) for i in range(5)]
results = ray.get(futures)

mean_acc = np.mean([r["val_acc"] for r in results])
print(f"Cross-validation accuracy: {mean_acc:.4f}")
```

## Ray Actors for Stateful Distributed Computing

```python
@ray.remote(num_gpus=1)
class ModelServer:
    """Stateful actor that loads a model once and serves predictions."""
    def __init__(self, model_path: str):
        self.device = torch.device("cuda:0")
        self.model = torch.load(model_path, map_location=self.device)
        self.model.eval()
        self.request_count = 0

    def predict(self, inputs: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        self.request_count += 1
        tensor = torch.from_numpy(inputs).to(self.device)
        with torch.no_grad():
            output = self.model(tensor)
        return output.cpu().numpy()

    def get_stats(self) -> dict:
        return {"requests_served": self.request_count}

# Create a pool of model servers
servers = [ModelServer.remote("model.pt") for _ in range(4)]

# Round-robin requests across servers
futures = []
for i, batch in enumerate(request_batches):
    server = servers[i % len(servers)]
    futures.append(server.predict.remote(batch))
predictions = ray.get(futures)
```

## Ray Train for Distributed Training

Ray Train abstracts away the complexity of distributed data-parallel training,
handling process group initialization, data sharding, and checkpointing.

```python
from ray.train.torch import TorchTrainer
from ray.train import ScalingConfig, RunConfig, CheckpointConfig
import ray.train.torch

def train_func(config):
    """Training function executed on each worker."""
    model = build_model(config["hidden_dim"])
    model = ray.train.torch.prepare_model(model)  # Wraps in DDP

    dataset = load_dataset(config["data_path"])
    dataloader = torch.utils.data.DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=config["batch_size"])
    dataloader = ray.train.torch.prepare_data_loader(dataloader)  # Auto-shards

    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=config["lr"])

    for epoch in range(config["epochs"]):
        for batch in dataloader:
            loss = model(batch)
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
            optimizer.zero_grad()

        # Report metrics (visible in Ray Dashboard)
        ray.train.report({"epoch": epoch, "loss": loss.item()})

trainer = TorchTrainer(
    train_loop_per_worker=train_func,
    train_loop_config={
        "data_path": "s3://bucket/data",
        "epochs": 50,
        "lr": 1e-4,
        "batch_size": 32,
        "hidden_dim": 1024,
    },
    scaling_config=ScalingConfig(
        num_workers=8,
        use_gpu=True,
        resources_per_worker={"GPU": 1, "CPU": 4},
    ),
    run_config=RunConfig(
        name="distributed-training",
        checkpoint_config=CheckpointConfig(num_to_keep=3),
    ),
)
result = trainer.fit()
print(f"Final loss: {result.metrics['loss']}")
```

## Ray Serve for Inference

```python
from ray import serve
import torch

@serve.deployment(
    num_replicas=4,
    ray_actor_options={"num_gpus": 1},
    max_concurrent_queries=100,
)
class LLMDeployment:
    def __init__(self, model_path: str):
        self.model = torch.load(model_path).cuda().eval()
        self.tokenizer = load_tokenizer()

    async def __call__(self, request):
        text = (await request.json())["text"]
        tokens = self.tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt").to("cuda")
        with torch.no_grad():
            output = self.model.generate(**tokens, max_new_tokens=256)
        return {"response": self.tokenizer.decode(output[0])}

app = LLMDeployment.bind(model_path="s3://models/llm-v2")
serve.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)
```

:::interactive{component="K8sVisualizer"}

## Dask for Scalable Data Processing

Dask extends NumPy arrays and Pandas DataFrames to distributed computing with
a familiar API. Dask builds a task graph lazily and executes it in parallel.

```python
import dask.dataframe as dd
import dask.array as da
from dask.distributed import Client

# Connect to Dask cluster
client = Client("tcp://scheduler:8786")
print(f"Dashboard: {client.dashboard_link}")

# Process terabytes of training data
df = dd.read_parquet("s3://data-lake/events/*.parquet")
features = (
    df.groupby("user_id")
      .agg({"event_count": "sum", "revenue": "mean", "session_time": "std"})
      .fillna(0)
)
features.to_parquet("s3://features/user_features/")

# Large matrix computation with Dask arrays
X = da.from_zarr("s3://data/embeddings.zarr")  # Shape: (10M, 768)
# Compute cosine similarity matrix in blocks
norms = da.linalg.norm(X, axis=1, keepdims=True)
X_normed = X / norms
similarity = da.dot(X_normed, X_normed.T)
# Get top-k nearest neighbors (computed lazily, executed in parallel)
top_k = similarity.topk(10, axis=1).compute()
```

## When to Use Ray vs Dask

| Feature | Ray | Dask |
|---------|-----|------|
| Primary use case | Distributed ML, serving, RL | Data processing, analytics |
| GPU support | First-class (training + serving) | Limited |
| Stateful computation | Actors | Limited |
| DataFrame API | Ray Data | dask.dataframe |
| Scheduling | Distributed, heterogeneous | Centralized |
| Best for | ML pipelines end-to-end | ETL and feature engineering |

exercises.title: Ray vs Traditional MPI
exercises.description: What is a key advantage of Ray over MPI for modern ML workloads?
options: Ray is fundamentally faster at AllReduce collective operations than NCCL over MPI
Ray provides higher-level APIs with automatic scheduling, fault tolerance, and heterogeneous resource management
Ray uses less GPU memory than MPI because of its shared object store
Ray only works on cloud instances while MPI only works on bare-metal HPC clusters
exercises.correct_answer: Ray provides higher-level APIs with automatic scheduling, fault tolerance, and heterogeneous resource management
hints: Think about developer experience: how much boilerplate does MPI require vs Ray?
Consider what happens when a worker fails in MPI vs Ray
exercises.solution: Ray provides higher-level APIs (remote functions, actors) with automatic task scheduling, built-in fault tolerance (task retries, actor restarts), and heterogeneous resource management (GPUs, CPUs, memory, custom resources). MPI requires manual process management, has no built-in fault tolerance (one process failure typically kills the entire job), and assumes homogeneous resources. Ray also unifies training, serving, and data processing in a single framework, while MPI is primarily designed for batch computation.

exercises.title: Distributed Hyperparameter Search with Ray
exercises.description: Write a Ray program that performs distributed hyperparameter search across 4 GPUs. Define a remote training function that takes a config dict (learning_rate, batch_size, hidden_dim), trains a model for 10 epochs, and returns validation accuracy. Launch 8 trials with different hyperparameter combinations and collect results to find the best configuration.
hints: Use @ray.remote(num_gpus=1) to pin each trial to one GPU
Use ray.put() to store the dataset once in the object store to avoid copying
Use ray.get() to collect all results, then find the best with max()
exercises.solution:
import ray
import numpy as np

ray.init()

@ray.remote(num_gpus=1)
def train_trial(data_ref, config: dict) -> dict:
    import torch
    data = ray.get(data_ref)
    device = torch.device(f"cuda:{ray.get_gpu_ids()[0]}")

    model = build_model(config["hidden_dim"]).to(device)
    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=config["lr"])

    for epoch in range(10):
        train_loss = train_epoch(model, data, optimizer, device, config["batch_size"])
        val_acc = evaluate(model, data, device)

    return {"config": config, "val_acc": val_acc}

# Store dataset once in shared memory
dataset = load_dataset()
data_ref = ray.put(dataset)

# Define 8 hyperparameter combinations
configs = [
    {"lr": lr, "batch_size": bs, "hidden_dim": hd}
    for lr in [1e-3, 1e-4]
    for bs in [32, 64]
    for hd in [256, 512]
]

# Launch all trials (Ray schedules them across 4 GPUs)
futures = [train_trial.remote(data_ref, c) for c in configs]
results = ray.get(futures)

best = max(results, key=lambda r: r["val_acc"])
print(f"Best config: {best['config']}, accuracy: {best['val_acc']:.4f}")

exercises.title: End-to-End ML Platform with Ray
exercises.description: Build a complete ML platform using Ray that includes: (1) A Dask-based data preprocessing pipeline that reads parquet files from S3 and generates features, (2) A Ray Train distributed training job that trains across 8 GPUs with automatic checkpointing, (3) A Ray Serve deployment that serves the trained model with 4 replicas and autoscaling, and (4) A monitoring integration that exposes custom Prometheus metrics for request latency and throughput.
hints: Use dask.dataframe.read_parquet for distributed data loading
Use TorchTrainer with ScalingConfig(num_workers=8, use_gpu=True) for training
Use @serve.deployment with ray_actor_options={'num_gpus': 1} for serving
Use prometheus_client to expose custom metrics from Ray Serve handlers
exercises.solution:
# 1. Dask data preprocessing
import dask.dataframe as dd
from dask.distributed import Client

client = Client("tcp://scheduler:8786")
df = dd.read_parquet("s3://data-lake/events/*.parquet")
features = df.groupby("user_id").agg({"clicks": "sum", "revenue": "mean"}).fillna(0)
features.to_parquet("s3://features/processed/")

# 2. Ray Train distributed training
from ray.train.torch import TorchTrainer
from ray.train import ScalingConfig, RunConfig, CheckpointConfig
import ray.train.torch

def train_func(config):
    model = build_model(config["hidden_dim"])
    model = ray.train.torch.prepare_model(model)
    dataloader = make_dataloader(config["data_path"], config["batch_size"])
    dataloader = ray.train.torch.prepare_data_loader(dataloader)
    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=config["lr"])
    for epoch in range(config["epochs"]):
        for batch in dataloader:
            loss = model(batch)
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
            optimizer.zero_grad()
        ray.train.report({"loss": loss.item(), "epoch": epoch})

trainer = TorchTrainer(
    train_loop_per_worker=train_func,
    train_loop_config={"data_path": "s3://features/processed/", "epochs": 50, "lr": 1e-4, "batch_size": 32, "hidden_dim": 1024},
    scaling_config=ScalingConfig(num_workers=8, use_gpu=True, resources_per_worker={"GPU": 1}),
    run_config=RunConfig(checkpoint_config=CheckpointConfig(num_to_keep=3)),
)
result = trainer.fit()

# 3. Ray Serve deployment with Prometheus metrics
from ray import serve
from prometheus_client import Histogram, Counter, start_http_server
import time

REQUEST_LATENCY = Histogram("inference_latency_seconds", "Inference latency")
REQUEST_COUNT = Counter("inference_requests_total", "Total requests")

@serve.deployment(num_replicas=4, ray_actor_options={"num_gpus": 1})
class ModelDeployment:
    def __init__(self, model_path):
        self.model = torch.load(model_path).cuda().eval()
        start_http_server(9090)  # Prometheus metrics endpoint

    async def __call__(self, request):
        REQUEST_COUNT.inc()
        with REQUEST_LATENCY.time():
            data = await request.json()
            tensor = torch.tensor(data["features"]).cuda()
            with torch.no_grad():
                prediction = self.model(tensor)
        return {"prediction": prediction.cpu().tolist()}

app = ModelDeployment.bind(model_path="model.pt")
serve.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)
